1: ʿal naharôt bābel šām yāšabnû gam-bākînû bĕzākorēnû ʾet-ṣiyyôn — "by the rivers of Babylon we sat down and wept when we remembered Zion" — naharôt bābel (rivers of Babylon) — the Euphrates and the network of canals and irrigation channels in the Babylonian plain; the city was defined by its waterways. The verb pairing yāšabnû gam-bākînû (we sat and also wept) — gam (also, even) heightens the emotional weight: the sitting was accompanied by weeping, not silence. bĕzākorēnû ʾet-ṣiyyôn (when we remembered Zion) — the memory of Zion triggers weeping; zākar (remember) is active emotional recollection, not passive nostalgia. The exiles sit at the Babylonian waterways — a spatial antitype of sitting beside the waters of Zion (Ps 46:4).
2: ʿal-ʿărābîm bĕtôkāh tālînû kinnōrôtênû — "we hung up our harps in the willow trees there" — ʿărābîm (willows, poplars; the Arabic-rooted word for "mixed stream" plant) — traditionally identified with willows or poplars along the riverbank. kinnōrôtênû (our lyres, not "harps") — the kinnôr is a small, hand-held stringed instrument, the instrument of David and Levitical temple music. Hanging the lyres on the trees is the gesture of musical suspension: the instruments are silenced. In the ancient Near East, hanging something high was a way of placing it beyond reach — the lyres were effectively retired while in exile.
3: kî šām šĕʾēlûnû šôbênû dibrê-šîr wĕtôlālênû śimḥāh šîrû lānû miššîr ṣiyyôn — "for there our captors demanded songs; those who ruined us wanted entertainment: 'Sing us a song of Zion!'" — šôbênû (our captors, those who took us captive) and tôlālênû (those who mocked/ruined us) — the double characterization of the oppressor. šîr ṣiyyôn (song of Zion) — a specific liturgical category celebrating Zion, Jerusalem, and YHWH's dwelling (Pss 46, 48, 76, 84, 87, 122). The captors' demand for Zion songs as entertainment is doubly cruel: the songs are sacred, the context profane; they are being asked to perform holy worship as spectacle for their conquerors.
4: ʾêk nāšîr ʾet-šîr-YHWH ʿal ʾadmat nēkār — "but how could we sing the LORD's song in a foreign land?" — ʾêk (how? — a question of impossibility, not mere difficulty) — the same word that opens Lamentations ("How does the city sit solitary…"). šîr-YHWH (song of the LORD) — not just a religious song but specifically the songs belonging to YHWH's liturgy. ʾadmat nēkār (soil of a foreigner/stranger) — from nākar (to treat as foreign, to be strange); the soil itself is alien. In ancient understanding, YHWH was particularly present in Israel's land; singing his liturgy on alien soil felt like a category violation.
5: ʾim-ʾeškāḥēk yĕrûšālāim tiškaḥ yĕmînî — "if I forget you, O Jerusalem, may my right hand lose all skill" — ʾeškāḥēk and tiškaḥ — both from šākaḥ (to forget) — the verb appears in both halves of the oath. yĕmînî tiškaḥ (let my right hand forget) — a self-imprecatory oath: if I forget Jerusalem, may my right hand lose its skill at lyre-playing — the very skill the captors demanded. The right hand is the skilled hand for music and warfare; its "forgetting" is its disabling. The psalmist stakes their musical identity on their fidelity to Jerusalem.
6: tidbaq-lĕšônî lĕḥikkî ʾim-lōʾ ʾezkĕrēkî ʾim-lōʾ ʾaʿăleh ʾet-yĕrûšālayim ʿal rōʾš śimḥātî — "if I do not hold you in memory, may my tongue stick to the roof of my mouth — if I do not place Jerusalem above my greatest delight" — tidbaq-lĕšônî lĕḥikkî (let my tongue stick to my palate) — the second self-imprecation, targeting the instrument of song; a silenced tongue cannot sing the captors' demanded songs. ʿal rōʾš śimḥātî (above/at the head of my joy) — Jerusalem is not merely important but first in rank among all delights. This is the covenantal hierarchy of desire: the love of Zion ranks above personal happiness.
7: zĕkōr YHWH libĕnê ʾĕdôm ʾēt yôm yĕrûšālāim hāʾōmĕrîm ʿārû ʿārû ʿad hayĕsôd bāh — "remember, O LORD, what Edom's sons did on the day Jerusalem fell — how they shouted, 'Tear it down! Tear it down to its very foundations!'" — zĕkōr YHWH (remember, O LORD) — in Hebrew this is a call to action, not merely mental recollection; the psalmist calls for YHWH to act in judgment as he witnessed. bĕnê ʾĕdôm (sons of Edom) — Edom's collaboration with Babylon's destruction of Jerusalem was a deep wound in the exilic community (Obad 10–14; Ezek 25:12–14; Lam 4:21–22). ʿārû ʿārû (tear it down! tear it down!) — the repetition echoes the crowd's jeering cry. hayĕsôd (the very foundation) — ʿad hayĕsôd bāh (to its bedrock): complete, total demolition.
8: bat-bābel hašĕdûdāh ʾašrê šeyyĕšallem-lāk ʾet-gĕmûlēk šeggāmalt lānû — "O Babylon, marked for destruction — how happy will be the one who pays you back for what you did to us!" — bat-bābel (daughter of Babylon) — the "daughter of" construction personifies a city as a woman, common in prophetic address (Jer 46:11; Lam 1:6; Zeph 3:14). hašĕdûdāh (the devastated, the one to be destroyed, Qal passive participle of šādad) — not yet destroyed but already addressed as condemned; prophetic judgment speaks the future as accomplished. gĕmûlēk (your repayment/recompense) — from gāmal (to deal out, repay, ripen); lex talionis applied to nations. ʾašrê (happy/blessed is) — the standard blessing formula (Ps 1:1) deployed in a strikingly harsh context; the one who carries out divinely-ordained retributive justice is declared "blessed."
9: ʾašrê šeyyōʾḥēz wĕnippēaṣ ʾet-ʿōlālayik ʾel-hassālaʿ — "blessed is he who takes your little ones and smashes them against the rocks" — ʿōlālayik (your little ones, your infants) — the same category in Isa 13:16 (Babylon's children dashed in the announced fall of Babylon) and Nah 3:10 (Nineveh's infants). The verse is among the most troubling in the Psalter and requires honest engagement. First, it is a lament, not a command — the psalmist does not act; they cry out. Second, it reflects lex talionis at the national level: Babylon had done precisely this to Jerusalem's children (2 Kgs 25:7; Lam 4:3–4); the prayer is for proportional retribution. Third, Isa 13:16 makes this the announced judgment of YHWH against Babylon — the psalm prays the fulfillment of prophetic decree. Fourth, the rage is honest: the psalm refuses to spiritualize grief. The imprecation is not commended as Christian virtue but preserved as truthful testimony to what exile does to human beings — and as a text that shows the Psalter capable of bearing even the ugliest human prayer before God.
